401C Unit 1

Pteridophytes

A cleaner, static, PDF-ready webpage version of the notes. This redesign avoids the earlier clutter: the numbering is small and visible, headings stay to the left, the text is larger and easier to read, and the palette uses multiple matte colours instead of only green. The life-cycle diagrams below are the original provided diagrams.

01 · General characteristics 02 · Life cycle 03 · Homosporic cycle 04 · Heterosporic cycle 05 · Apogamy 06 · Apospory
Vegetative morphology and anatomy
01

General characteristics

All ten characteristics are kept in one continuous serial section. The topics on spore types, prothallus and fertilization are included here as points 7–10, so the structure matches the source material more naturally.

1

Dominant Sporophyte Generation

Unlike bryophytes, the main, independent and long-lived plant body of a pteridophyte is a diploid sporophyte (2n), which is differentiated into true roots, a true stem (often a rhizome), and true leaves.

2

First Vascular Cryptogams

They are the first terrestrial plants to evolve specialized vascular tissues for conduction. However, their xylem typically lacks vessels (consisting mainly of tracheids), and their phloem lacks companion cells and sieve tubes (possessing sieve cells instead), with a few exceptions like Selaginella or Pteridium.

3

Leaf Dimorphism

Leaves are generally classified into two anatomical types: microphylls (small leaves with a single, unbranched vascular strand and no leaf gaps, as seen in Lycopodium) and megaphylls (large, broad leaves with complex, branched venation and leaf gaps, known as fronds, as seen in true ferns).

4

Primary Root Replacement

The primary root of the sporophyte is short-lived and is soon replaced by an adventitious root system that arises from the stem.

5

Sporangia and Sporophylls

Asexual reproduction occurs via haploid spores produced inside multicellular structures called sporangia. These sporangia are typically borne on the abaxial (lower) surface or axils of specialized fertile leaves known as sporophylls.

6

Sori and Strobili Formation

In many true ferns, sporangia aggregate into distinct clusters called sori, which may be protected by a specialized flap of tissue called an indusium. In lycophytes and horsetails, sporophylls compactly aggregate at the stem apices to form distinct cones or strobili.

Spore types, prothallus and fertilization

7

Homospory vs. Heterospory

While the majority of pteridophytes are homosporous (producing only one morphological type of spore), several highly advanced genera are heterosporous (producing smaller microspores and larger megaspores). Heterospory in genera like Selaginella and Marsilea is considered a crucial evolutionary precursor to the seed habit.

8

Independent Gametophyte (Prothallus)

Spores germinate to form a small, inconspicuous, multicellular and free-living haploid gametophyte called a prothallus. In homosporous species, the prothallus is exosporic (growing outside the spore wall) and photosynthetic; in heterosporous species, it is endosporic (developing within the spore wall) and non-green.

9

Water Dependency for Zoidogamy

The prothallus bears multicellular, jacketed sex organs: the antheridia (male) and archegonia (female). Because antheridia produce flagellated, motile antherozoids, water is an absolute requirement for them to swim to the archegonial neck and fertilize the egg. This limits pteridophytes to predominantly moist, damp and shady ecological niches.

10

Heteromorphic Alternation of Generations

The life cycle exhibits a distinct heteromorphic alternation of generations. A dominant, complex diploid sporophyte alternates with a morphologically distinct, simple haploid gametophyte, with both generations living independently of one another at maturity.

Life cycle of pteridophytes
02

Alternation of generations

There is a regular heteromorphic alternation of generation, i.e. sporophytic phase (2n) alternates with the gametophytic phase (n).

The sporophytic plant body of pteridophyte is differentiated into roots, stems and leaves. The sporophytic plant on maturity bears spore-bearing organs commonly called sporangia or strobilus.

At the time of sporogenesis the reduction division takes place and the haploid spores (n) are formed. These haploid spores are the beginning of the gametophytic generation (n).

The spores on germination/meiosis develop a gametophytic plant body known as the prothallus (plural = prothalli) in vascular cryptogams. It is a minute and microscopic structure.

The prothallus as a result of meiosis division further develops the sex organs — the male one is called antheridium (plural = antheridia), while the female is called archegonium (plural = archegonia). The antheridia and archegonia bear the male gametes called antherozoids and the female gametes called egg or ovum, respectively. The male gametes are flagellate and motile, while female gametes are non-motile. Inside each globose-shaped antheridium a large number of antherozoids (n) are produced, while in each archegonium only a single egg or ovum (n) is developed in a flask-shaped archegonium.

A haploid antherozoid and an egg, as a result of union or fertilization, produce the resultant diploid cell (2n) called zygote. Zygote is the beginning of sporophytic generation (2n), which on germination/development gives rise to a new sporophytic plant body. Thus, these two phases follow one another in the life-cycle, in regular succession.

Type A
03

Homosporic alternation of generation

In some members of pteridophytes all spores produced by the sporangia are of the same type, and in that case, such a plant is known as homosporous (e.g. Lycopodium, Equisetum, etc.).

The spores are haploid (n) and are the beginning of the gametophytic generation (n). The spores on germination develop gametophytes or prothallus. It is a minute and microscopic structure. The prothalli of homosporic pteridophytes are always monoecious, i.e. they bear both male and female sex organs in a single plant/prothallus.

The prothallus, at maturity, develops the sex organs — the male one is called antheridium, while the female one is called archegonium. The antheridia bear the male gametes called antherozoids and the archegonia bear female gamete called egg or ovum. The male gametes are flagellate and motile, while female gametes are non-motile. Inside each globose-shaped antheridium a large number of antherozoids (n) are produced, while only a single egg or ovum (n) is developed in the flask-shaped archegonium.

Original diagram · Homosporic life cycle of pteridophytes

Original provided diagram used directly for accuracy.

A haploid antherozoid and an egg, as a result of union, fertilization is effected and the resulted diploid cell (2n) is called zygote. Zygote is the beginning of sporophytic generation (2n) which on germination/development gives rise to a new sporophytic plant body. Thus, these two phases follow one another in the life-cycle of homosporic pteridophytes, in regular succession.

Type B
04

Heterosporic alternation of generation

On the other hand, the heterosporous pteridophytes (e.g. Selaginella, Marsilea, etc.) bear two types of spores.

Smaller spores are known as microspores or male spores and are developed in microsporangia, while the larger spores are known as megaspores or female spores and are formed in megasporangia.

Both the microspores and megaspores are haploid (n). The microspores on germination produce the male gametophytes on which antheridia are developed and inside each antheridium a large number of sperms or antherozoids are produced. The megaspores produce the female gametophytes on which archegonia are developed and inside each archegonium a single egg or ovum is produced. Thus the gametophytes or prothalli of heterosporic pteridophytes are always dioecious.

Original diagram · Heterosporic life cycle of pteridophytes

Original provided diagram used directly for accuracy.

A haploid antherozoid and an egg, as a result of union, fertilization is effected and the resulted diploid cell (2n) is called zygote. Zygote is the beginning of sporophytic generation (2n) which on germination/development gives rise to a new sporophytic plant body. Thus, these two phases follow one another in the life-cycle of heterosporic pteridophytes, in regular succession.

Homosporic

One type of spore. Prothallus is usually monoecious. Examples: Lycopodium, Equisetum.

Heterosporic

Two spore types: microspores and megaspores. Prothalli are dioecious. Examples: Selaginella, Marsilea.

Special phenomena
05

Apogamy

Apogamy is the development of sporophyte directly from the vegetative cells of the gametophyte without the intervention of sex organs, i.e. without gametic union or syngamy.

Thus apogamy is the development of an embryo from a cell of the gametophyte other than egg cell or zygote. The embryo so formed develops into the sporophyte.

Apogamy was first reported by Farlow (1874) in Pteris cretica. It is a constant phenomenon in a number of ferns and has also been introduced under experimental conditions, such as strong light, modified nutrient culture solutions and insufficient water supply for fertilization. Natural apogamy has been reported in more than 50 species of ferns belonging to 20 genera. In some species of ferns apogamy appears to be a necessity and is a regular process. It is perhaps due to the inherited characters of the plant. The natural apogamy commonly occurs in Dryopteris, Pteris, Pellaea, Adiantum, Osmunda, Todea, Polystichum, Asplenium, etc. Apart from ferns apogamy has also been induced in some species of Lycopodium, Equisetum, etc.

Causes and artificial induction of apogamy

Regarding the causes of apogamy several explanations have been put forward. Lang (1898) induced the formation of sporophytic buds, roots, sporangia, etc. in various fern prothalli by avoiding watering of the prothalli. Brown (1923) summarized literature regarding the induction of apogamy by avoiding fertilization of the egg. According to many pteridologists, failure of normal fertilization results in the apogamous production of sporophyte. Mottier (1931), however, demonstrated that in Matteuccia struthiopteris failure of fertilization does not induce apogamy. Brown (1923) induced apogamy in Phegopteris polypodioides by avoiding normal fertilization. Other conditions favouring apogamy are culture in bright light and at higher temperatures. Williams (1938) suggested that in addition to environmental factors there may also be some internal factors such as the nature of inherent susceptibility due to abnormal nuclear composition and behaviour which bring about apogamy.

Ageing of the prothallus has also been regarded as one of the factors influencing apogamous developments on the prothalli of some ferns. Whittier and Steeves (1960) showed that apogamy can be induced by growing the prothalli on an agar culture medium rich in glucose. De Maggio (1964) induced the formation of sporophyte buds on the prothalli of Lycopodium obscurum grown in culture media containing coconut milk and sucrose. These experiments reveal the effect of nutritional media in inducing apogamy.

Quick recall: Apogamy skips syngamy and produces a sporophyte directly from gametophytic tissue.
Special phenomena
06

Apospory

Apospory is the development of gametophyte directly from the vegetative cells of the sporophyte without the intervention of spores.

In apospory, a filamentous or heart-shaped gametophyte/prothallus may be formed from one or more cells of any vegetative portion of a young or mature sporophyte. The prothallus so formed bears antheridia and archegonia.

The phenomenon of apospory was first discovered by Druery (1884) in a fern called Athyrium filix-femina var. clarissima. He reported the development of prothalli from the stalk of the sporangium as well as from the spore case of this fern. Later apospory was noticed in many ferns and some workers induced it in cultural conditions.

Druery and Nurenberg (1938) and Steil (1939, 1951) reviewed the literature on apospory and apogamy in ferns. Wetmore and De Maggio (1963) have also reviewed cases of apospory and apogamy among ferns. Beyerle (1932) reported the development of prothalli on leaf tips and shoot buds at the basal and older parts of the leaf in Davallia canariensis and Nephrolepis biserata.

Causes and artificial induction of apospory

Several factors seem to be responsible for the aposporous development of gametophytes from vegetative tissue of the sporophyte. Briston (1962) demonstrated that mineral nutrition is responsible for the formation of prothalli from callus tissue obtained from the sporophyte of the fern Pteris cretica. This callus developed into gametophytes when he supplied sucrose. In some ferns like Drymaria rigudula, Polypodium aureum and P. heracleum the leaves develop prothalli under dim-light and sporophytic buds under strong light. Koehler (1920) demonstrated that in Platycerium bifurcatum the leaves develop prothalli grown under dim-light whereas the same leaves produce sporophytic buds on exposure to strong light. Goebel (1907) and Palta (1973) induced the formation of prothalli from the petiole and laminar surfaces of the pinnae in Pteris vittata.

Brown (1918) reported an interesting case of regeneration from the petioles of the leaves of Phegopteris polypodioides. Under experimental conditions a cellular mass of green cells developed on the cultured petioles. It later developed into a prothallus bearing rhizoids, true leaves, and structure intermediate between leaves and prothallia. Morlang (1967) induced apospory in three species of Asplenium. Takahashi (1962) induced apospory in Pteridium aquilinum var. latiusculum. He was able to induce the growth of gametophyte from petiolar epidermis, from margins of pinnae, from epidermal cells, from mesophyll cells and from root epidermis.

Quick recall: Apospory skips spores and produces a gametophyte directly from sporophytic tissue.

Static single-file HTML · no scripts · better suited for export to PDF.
